topic: Someone learns their favorite author plagiarized their stories
raw_output:
[story 1]

The first time I saw it, I almost dismissed it as a coincidence, a mere stylistic echo. My hands, usually steady when I held a well-worn paperback, trembled slightly as I reread the passage. It was a scene, exquisitely rendered, a moment of quiet despair that felt intimately familiar. Then, the pattern emerged. A specific sequence of metaphors, a unique turn of phrase describing a character’s internal conflict—it was lifted, polished, and placed into a new narrative. A cold knot tightened in my chest, a heavy, suffocating pressure. I closed the book, the sound of the cover hitting the table echoing too loudly in the quiet apartment. I spent the next hour tracing the borrowed lines, a strange mixture of hurt and a profound sense of witnessing an injustice washing over me. I felt a deep, aching sympathy for the author whose voice had been so carefully stolen.

[story 2]

"It’s uncanny," I murmured, the words catching in my throat. I leaned closer to the screen, the blue light reflecting off my wide eyes. The novel I was analyzing, the one I adored, contained a chapter whose emotional core mirrored a story I’d written years ago, a piece I’d poured my very soul into. Every carefully constructed sentence felt like a betrayal. My jaw tightened, and I pressed the heels of my palms against my eyes, trying to physically push the intrusion away. It wasn't just plagiarism; it felt like a theft of a private memory. A wave of sorrow washed over me, making my breath shallow. I looked at the author’s name on the dedication page, and a strange, protective instinct flared within me—a fierce desire to shield someone who had been so vulnerable, so exposed by this act.

[story 3]

I sat by the window, the city lights blurring into streaks of indifferent color. The manuscript lay open, the borrowed words glaring back at me. I didn't move for a long time, just letting the silence amplify the feeling of wrongness. When I finally reached out, my fingers hovered over the text, tracing the copied phrases with a feather-light touch, as if afraid the paper itself might crumble under the weight of the transgression. A deep, resonant sadness settled in my bones, a heavy cloak of shared vulnerability. I felt a powerful urge to speak, to confront the situation, but the words wouldn't form. Instead, I simply sighed, a long, shaky exhalation that seemed to carry the entire weight of the narrative’s stolen heart. It was a quiet acknowledgment of the pain inflicted, a silent recognition of a kindred spirit wronged.
